subject: social_sciences
chapter_title: Chapter 8: Building Blocks in Economics: The Problem of Choice

competencies.justification: The chapter's fundamental organizing purpose is the problem of choice worked out through production and distribution decisions and the factors that influence them. Its definitional core ('What does Economics Deal with?') defines the discipline through production, distribution, demand, prices, and the influence of technology, and three consecutive dedicated sections build directly on this: 'Key Questions in Economics' frames what/how/for whom to produce; 'What to Produce and for Whom?' analyses production and distribution decisions (sugarcane vs millets, shoes for different consumer groups); and 'How to Produce?' analyses labour- versus capital-intensive methods and the factors of production — land, labour, capital, and technology (Fig. 8.6). The end-of-chapter exercises most frequently demand exactly this reasoning — Q1 links changing wants to production, Q6 and Q7 turn on opportunity cost in production choices, and Q10 asks students to analyse a real production/supply decision — so Economics governs the chapter's primary structural activity. Complete removal of the competency defining production, distribution, and their influencing factors would structurally dissolve the chapter's organising purpose, qualifying it for Weight 3 under Rules 4 and 7; where CG-7 and CG-8 both approach the Weight 3 test, the definitional problem-of-choice and production core reserves the single Weight 3 for C-7.1 under Rule 8.

The chapter constructs a dedicated, architecturally distinct block, 'Economic Systems and How Choices are Made,' whose three sub-sections — Planned Economy (state-controlled allocation and prices), Market Economy (allocation by demand and supply, private ownership), and Mixed Economy (both combined, with India post-1991, China, Germany, and Sweden as examples, developed further in the DON'T MISS OUT box) — map precisely onto C-8.2's 'range of economic systems — from free market to entirely state-controlled markets.' This block could stand alone as a learning unit, and the end-of-chapter exercises Q4 (which system gives the most freedom and best promotes innovation) and Q5 (why pure systems rarely exist and why a mixed economy is more practical) compel students to analyse the concepts and practice of these systems. This satisfies Rule 5(a) for Weight 2; as the second Economics competency approaching the Weight 3 test, it is held at Weight 2 under Rule 8 behind the chapter's central production-and-choice purpose (Rule 7 does not bar it, both codes being within the governing Economics sub-discipline).

Within 'What to Produce and for Whom?' a designated passage develops the sugarcane-versus-millets decision explicitly as a trade-off between short-term economic gains and long-term sustainability — the opportunity cost of sugarcane's profits being the forgone saving of water and improvement of soil health — requiring the student to weigh economic development against environmental well-being. This is a single named passage whose content requires that cognitive operation rather than a dedicated section developing the economy-environment connection, meeting the Weight 1 condition under Rule 6; it does not qualify for a higher weight, as the chapter's primary structural activity is the problem of choice in production, not the appreciation of well-being beyond GDP.
